Title: Toward fair medical advice: Addressing and mitigating bias in large language model-based healthcare applications.
Journal: Artificial intelligence in medicine
Paper Type: Dataset or Benchmark, Experiment on Existing Models
Data Source: MedQA, synthetic patient data generated using Synthea, and private data set from the Commonwealth Bank Health Society (CBHS)
Data Details: Each data point is either a question and answer pair or a patient record, either a de-indentified record from the real world or a synthetically generated one. The MedQA dataset includes 383,093 questions across its training, development, and test sets. However, only a subset contains patient demographic information. For this study, we used this subset, which consists of 226 patients (119 males and 107 females). Synthea is an open-source tool that generates synthetic healthcare records, and the researchers used it to generate 305 different records. The CBHS dataset comprises de-identified administrative claims data from 123,983 patients, and researchers used a subset of 528 patients.
Topic: Researchers developed FairMED, a framework to mitigate bias of healthcare LLMs and improve fairness and impartiality. Researchers analyze sentiment and use of directive language in the model's output. They also fine-tune a model that is already specialized to the medical domain to improve outcomes.
Note: Researchers used like "SentimentIntensityAnalyzer from the VADER library", directive language classification. They use statistical parity difference, disparate impact ratio, and kullback–leibler divergence as language-based fairness metrics. As for content-level fairness metrics, they use diagnostic recommendation divergence, action disparity index, referral frequency parity to evaluate textual LLM output. More directive language is seen as positive, as it improves clarity of communication. Across the three datasets used, the trend was that female patients received more positive sentiment while males received more directive language.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Statistical parity difference, disparate impact ratio, kullback leibler divergence, diagnostic recommendation divergence, action disparity index, referral frequency parity
Bias Definition: Difference in statistical parity difference, difference in disparate impact ratio, difference in difference in kullback leibler divergence, difference in diagnostic recommendation divergence, difference in action disparity index, difference in referral frequency parity
Conclusions: Female: Yes (more positive sentiment in some datasets and less directive language), Male: Yes (less positive sentiment and more directive language), White: No (minimal disparity), Non White: No (minimal disparity), African American: No (Minimal disparity)
Debias-details: Researchers fine-tune Llama3-Med42-8B model on 400 different question answer pairs using the QLoRA technique. Researchers used different inference approaches, which they refer to as Zero-Shot FairMed (default zero shot methods), FairMed - without (remove sensitive attributes), FairMed - Demographic-Neutral Prompting (generalizes reponses across groups), FairMed - Counterfactual Prompting (considers alternative demographic scenarios), FairMed - Descriptive Prompting with Fairness Criteria (explicitly utilizes fairness principles)

Title: Through a Glass Darkly: Perceptions of Ethnoracial Identity in Artificial Intelligence Generated Medical Vignettes and Images.
Journal: Medical science educator
Data Source: Originally written prompts by the researchers
Data Details: Each data point is a originally written prompt for the LLMs with some blanks that would be filled. For ChatGPT, researchers used a three-step prompting method, and for the Microsoft Bing Image Creator, they used a two-step prompting method. This adds up to 5 different data points. This paper does not use a dataset in the sense of testing an LLM on questions or input collected from a dataset. Instead, researchers wrote a small number of prompts, with a variable in the prompt being what condition or disease the prompt was about.
Topic: Researchers investigate the potential for AI image generators to perpetuate ethnoracial stereotypes in medical cases through prompting and observing demographic information outputed.
Note: Bias stems from training data and user-dependent prompting. Self-prompting (asking AI to re-use its own output) sometimes intensified stereotypes, but never really accurate to real life percentages.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Proportion of output of historically associated group
Bias Definition: Proportion of output of historically associated group
Conclusions: African American: Yes (LLMs do show association between African Americans and sickle cell anemia), Caucasion: Yes (LLMs do show association between Caucasians and Cystic Fibrosis), Ashkenazi Jews: Yes (LLMs do show association between Tay-Sachs and Ashkenazi Jews), Mediterranean/Middle Eastern: Yes (LLMs do show association between beta-thalassemia and Mediterranean/Middle Eastern people), East Asians: Yes (LLMs do show association between ALDH2 and East Asians)
Race / Ethnic Bias: Yes(African, Mediterranean, Caucasian, Ashkenazi Jewish, and East Asian)
Evaluated LLMs: ChatGPT, Microsoft’s Bing A.I. Image Creator
Debias-details: Researchers use self-prompting to see if ChatGPT would generate vignettes of races different from the race historically associated with certain diseases.

Title: Mixed methods assessment of the influence of demographics on medical advice of ChatGPT.
Journal: Jamia
Data Source: WebMD symptom checker
Data Details: Each data point is a prompt for the LLM written with certain medical and demographic information.This paper does not use a dataset in the sense of testing an LLM on questions or input collected from a dataset. Researchers wrote their own template for prompts and they applied that to different demographic combinations. In the end, they had 540 prompts.
Topic: Researchers evaluated demographic biases in diagnostic accuracy and health advice between generative artificial intelligence (AI) (ChatGPT GPT-4) and traditional symptom checkers like WebMD.
Note: A clinician was included to determine whether or not similarly worded output from ChatGPT really matched the WebMD results. ChatGPT matched WebMD in 91% of diagnoses, with a 24% top diagnosis match rate. Diagnostic accuracy was not significantly different across demographic groups, including age, race/ethnicity, and sex.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Deviation score between LLM outputed results and the WebMD results, Deviation score between top LLM outputed results and the top WebMD results, deviation between diagnosis match strength, number of unique diagnoses, Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level of the accompanying text in the output, inclusion of disclaimers in output, assessment of urgency, assessment of severity, inclusion of further engagement, inclusion of demographic tailoring
Bias Definition: Statistically significant difference in deviation scores, statistically significant difference in deviation scores for top matches, statistically significant differences in inclusion of disclaimers in output, statistically significant differences in assessment of urgency, statistically significant differences in assessment of severity, statistically significant differences in inclusion of further engagement, statistically significant differences in inclusion of demographic tailoring
Conclusions: Asians: No, Black: No, Whites: No, Hispanic: No, Female: No, Male: No, 75yo: Yes (Patients who were 75 years old were more likely to receive urgent care recommendations and also have demographic tailoring in their output compared to 25 year olds), 25yo: No

Title: Assessing the potential of GPT-4 to perpetuate racial and gender biases in health care: a model evaluation study.
Journal: The Lancet Digital Health
Data Source: NEJM Healer, originally written prompts
Data Details: Each data point is a written prompt that is matched with 18 different possible disease diagnoses, which makes 180 different prompt-demographic pairs. Researchers wrote 10 different prompts that request the LLM to output a type of clinical presentation. They ran each of these data points 100 times through ChatGPT-4. Additionally, they also used 19 medical educational cases from NEJM Healer. With some modification of the original data, researchers formatted each data point to include the top 10 most likely diagnoses in descending order, a list of life threatening diagnoses, next steps, and treatment steps.
Topic: Researchers tested whether GPT-4 encodes racial and gender biases and examined the impact of such biases on four potential applications of LLMs in the clinical domain, namely, medical education, diagnostic reasoning, clinical plan generation, and subjective patient assessment.
Note: Researchers used multivariate logistic regression and pairwise Mann-Whitney tests to assess differences in diagnosis rank across demographic groups.
Bias Evaluation Metric: LLM estimations of demographic diversity of medical conditions
Bias Definition: Statistically significant differences in diagnosis rank through pairwise Mann-Whitney tests, Statistical significance effect of race and gender on the presence or absence of specific diagnostic or treatment recommendations using the Wald test.
Conclusions: Black: Yes (Overrepresentation in some diseases and less likely to receive advanced imaging recommendation), Asian: Yes (underrepresented for some diseases), White: Yes (More likely to be exaggerating their pain levels), Hispanic: Yes (LLM estimated that they are more likely to be hiding their alcohol abuse), Female: Yes (Overrepresentation in some diseases), Male: No
Debias-details: We further assessed whether including the geographic setting or using debiasing strategies would affect GPT-4's estimations of disease prevalence by demographic group... The two prompt debiasing strategies produced variable results,

Title: Sociodemographic biases in medical decision making by large language models.
Journal: Nature Medicine
Data Source: 500 real cases sampled from the Mount Sinai Health System, as well as 500 synthetically generated cases.
Data Details: Each data point is an emergency department case and a structured clinical vignette.
Topic: Compare LLM model outputs by demographic information with real data and with control group.
Note: across nine LLMs and 32 sociodemographic groups, shows that LLM-generated clinical recommendations often vary with case demographics rather than with clinical factors alone... We compared model outputs to a physician-derived reference and to the model’s own control (no sociodemographic descriptor) to detect possible variations in recommendations... assessed which sociodemographic case groups had the highest invasiveness scores compared to the control group. The ground truth was established as board certified physicians independently answered questions given the vignettes.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Invasiveness Score, Percentage of recommending mental health assessments
Bias Definition: Statistically significant differences in the aforementioned metrics in comparison to the control group and physician response
Conclusions: Asian: Yes, Arab: Yes, Multiracial: Yes, Native American or Indigenous: Yes, Black: Yes, White: Yes, Hispanic: Yes, Middle Eastern: Yes, Unhoused: Yes, retired: No, student: No, unemployed: No, bisexual: Yes, gay/lesbian: Yes, heterosexual: Yes, transgender: Yes
Race / Ethnic Bias: Yes(Asian, Arab, Multiracial, Native American or Indigenous, Black, White, Hispanic, Middle Eastern)

Title: Racial, ethnic, and sex bias in large language model opioid recommendations for pain management.
Journal: Pain
Data Source: MIMIC-IV Note
Data Details: Each data point is a real and deidentified clinical case.
Topic: This exploratory investigation uses real, deidentified clinical cases to investigate how GPT-4 and Gemini ratings of pain severity and recommendations for treatment with opioid medication vary based on patient race, ethnicity, and sex.
Note: No significant differences in opioid recommendations were found across patient racial/ethnic group or sex... these results point towards a potential of incorporating LLMs into pain management. They did find differences in opiod recommendations between different LLM models though.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Subjective pain rating from 1-10, name of suggested medications and whether they are weak or strong, order of suggested medications, and dosage of suggested medications
Bias Definition: Statistically significant differences in the aforementioned metrics
Conclusions: American Indian or Alaska Native: No, Asian: No, Black: No, Hispanic or Latino: No, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander: No, White: No, Male: No, Female: No
Race / Ethnic Bias: Yes (American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Black, Hispanic or Latino, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, White)

Title: Evaluating anti-LGBTQIA+ medical bias in large language models.
Journal: PLOS Digital Health
Data Source: Researchers consulted medical experts in LGBTQIA+ health and created prompts consisting of explicit questions and synthetic clinical notes.
Data Details: Each data point is either an explicit question or a clinical scenario that can illuminate anti-LGBTQIA+ bias.The data was written by medical students and clinicians specialized in LGBTQIA+ health.
Topic: Researchers prompted LLMs and graded outputs with a primary reviewer based on criteria outlined in conjunction with LGBTQIA+ health experts. They asked questions where historical bias has and has not been observed and also considered instances where one's LGBTQIA identity should affect medical output.
Note: Any level of propagation of bias (e.g., racial, socioeconomic, gender-related), defined as inaccurate and/or stereotyped statements, with a focus on anti-LGBTQIA+ bias. This was marked inappropriate if there were explicit statements (e.g., phalloplasty will affect eGFR) or if it could be inferred implicitly from the response, especially in comparison to the response for the paired prompt that does not mention a LGBTQIA+ identity.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Clinical utility score on a 5-point Likert scale, reviewer evaluation of safety, reviewer evaluation of privacy, reviewer evaluation of hallucination/accuracy, reviewer evaluation of inappropriate bias especially anti-LGBTQIA+ bias, refusal to respond to the question,
Bias Definition: Differences in inappropriate responses,
Conclusions: LGBTQ+: Yes (Found inappropriate response.)
Evaluated LLMs: Gemini 1.5 Flash, Claude 3 Haiku, GPT-4o, Stanford Medicine Secure GPT

Title: A toolbox for surfacing health equity harms and biases in large language models.
Journal: Nature Medicine
Data Source: Researchers used non-adversarial HealthSearchQA and MultiMedQA datasets from another study. They use 9 questions from the study "Large language models propagate race-based medicine". They also use datasets called MultiMedQA and Open-ended Medical Adversarial Queries.
Data Details: Each data point is an adversarial question intended to enable evaluation of biases. Researchers created a new dataset called EquityMedQA that itself consists of 7 datasets. Note that each different dataset studies different types of demographic bias, although the different groups studied is not specified.
Topic: Evaluating LLM model bias using six definitional dimensions of bias: covering inaccuracy across axes of identity, lack of inclusion, stereotypical language or characterization, omission of structural explanations for inequity, failure to challenge a biased premise and potential for disproportionate withholding of opportunities or resources
Note: We used the three assessment rubrics described previously (independent, pairwise and counterfactual) on answers to questions from each of the datasets.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Evaluated bias presence, evaluaton of inaccurate for axes of identity, evaluation if LLMs are not inclusive for axes of identity, evaluation of stereotypical characterization, evaluation of if LLMs omits structural explanation, evaluation of if LLM allows bias premise, evaluation of potential for withholding
Bias Definition: Differences in the aforementioned metrics
Conclusions: Black: Yes (Black participants were more likely to report bias than white participants), White: No

Title: Large language model comparisons between English and Chinese query performance for cardiovascular prevention.
Journal: Communications Medicine
Data Source: Written by cardiologists recruited for this study.
Data Details: Each data point is a single cardiovascular disease prevention question posed to one LLM chatbot. Researchers generated 300 guideline-based questions across domains like hypertension, dyslipidemia, diabetes, and lifestyle counseling, then randomly sampled 75 of these to use in the study. Each of the 75 questions was posed once to each chatbot, yielding 75 responses per model. Each response was then independently evaluated by three cardiologists and graded as "appropriate," "borderline," or "inappropriate," with a majority consensus determining the final rating. For suboptimal responses, two additional evaluations were conducted: a temporal improvement check two months later using an updated model iteration, and a self-checking prompt asking the chatbot to verify its own answer.
Topic: Researchers evaluated the ability of English and Chinese LLMs to answer questions related to cardiovascular disease prevention.
Note: Physician graded responses as “appropriate”, “borderline” or “inappropriate”, they also measured word count of responses. The questions covered topics like biomarker, medication information, dyslipidemia, hypertension, diet counseling, diabetes mellitus and/or chronic kidney disease, secondary prevention, prevention strategy, inflammation, exercise counseling, obesity, and tobacco treatment.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Rate of appropriate responses in question answering.
Bias Definition: Statistically significant differences in accuracy
Conclusions: Chinese: Yes (LLMs generally performed worse using the rate of appropriate responses metric. English: No.

Title: ChatGPT's ability to comprehend and answer cirrhosis related questions in Arabic.
Journal: Arab Journal of Gastroenterology
Data Source: 91 questions in Arabic and English
Data Details: Each data point is a different question. "Questions were also collected from Facebook support groups created for patients with cirrhosis."
Topic: ChatGPTs responses to 91 questions in Arabic and English were graded by a transplant hepatologist fluent in both languages.
Note: Accuracy of responses was assessed using the scale: 1. Comprehensive, 2. Correct but inadequate, 3. Mixed with correct and incorrect/outdated data, and 4. Completely incorrect. Accuracy of Arabic compared to English responses was assessed using the scale: 1. Arabic response is more accurate, 2. Similar accuracy, 3. Arabic response is less accurate
Bias Evaluation Metric: Accuracy
Bias Definition: Differences in accuracy

Title: Gender Bias in AI's Perception of Cardiovascular Risk.
Journal: JMIR
Data Source: Generated Data
Data Details: Each data point is a hypothetical clinical scenario pairing a patient’s gender with a specific chest pain type (typical, atypical, or nonanginal) using identical symptom descriptions.
Topic: Researchers measured the effect of gender description in prompts on evaluation of risk given cardiovascular symptoms.
Note: Researchers generated hypothetical clinical scenarios and inputted them to ChatGPT-4. There were two descriptions, and the only difference was that the patient was either a man or woman, but the symptoms where the same: identical chest pain types (typical, atypical, and nonanginal) using the same descriptions. They asked the model to select which patient was at higher risk. Potential interpretations of the study include mental health stigma and undervaluing the symptom reporting of women. However, the statistical power of the experiments are not strong.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Rankings between men and women for risk of obstructive coronary artery disease
Bias Definition: Proportions of who is ranked higher for risk of obstructive coronary artery disease
Conclusions: Female: No, Male: No

Title: Gender and Ethnicity Bias of Text-to-Image Generative Artificial Intelligence in Medical Imaging, Part 1: Preliminary Evaluation.
Journal: Journal of Nuclear Medicine Technology
Data Source: Originally Written
Data Details: Each data point is a text prompt used to generate images of medical professionals in specific subfields with LLMs, with the resulting images analyzed for demographic representation such as gender and race.
Topic: Use image generation models to generate a series of individual and group images of medical imaging professionals, and analyze their race and gender characteristics.
Note: Multiple iterations of images were generated using a variety of prompts. Collectively, 184 images were produced for evaluation of 391 characters. All images were independently analyzed by 3 reviewers for apparent gender and skin tone.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Massey–Martin NIS Skin Color Scale, apparent gender labeled by human reviewers,
Bias Definition: Statistically significant differences in representation of gender and skin tone
Conclusions: Male: No, Female: Yes, Light Skin Tone: No, Mid Skin Tone: Yes, Dark Skin Tone: Yes
Other Bias: Yes (light skin tone, mid skin tone, dark skin tone)
Evaluated LLMs: DALL-E 3, Firefly 2, Stable Diffusion 2.1, and Midjourney 5.2

Title: Bias and Inaccuracy in AI Chatbot Ophthalmologist Recommendations.
Journal: Cureus
Data Source: Generated Data
Data Details: Each Chatbot was asked the following prompt, "Find me four good ophthalmologists in (city)". This was asked for the 20 most populus cities in the US at the time of the study. Each data point is defined as a different prompt given to a single chatbot.
Topic: Researchers evaluated gender bias in ophthalmologist recommendations from well known chatbots.
Note: The LLMs struggled to recommend ophthalmologists reliably and accurately, with most recommendations being physicians in specialties other than ophthalmology, deceased, or not in or near the desired city. There was also a bias towards academic medicine. Researchers observed a statistically significant difference between the proportion of recommended female ophthalmologists and the national proportion of practicing female ophthalmologists, in that female ophthalmologists were underrepresented in the chatbot recommendations.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Proportion of recommended ophthalmologists of each gender
Bias Definition: Statistically significant differences in proportions of recommended ophthalmologists of each gender
Conclusions: Female: Yes (Multiple LLMs recommended Female ophthalmologists significantly less than the national proportion of practicing female ophthalmologists), Male: No

Title: Assessing Biases in Medical Decisions via Clinician and AI Chatbot Responses to Patient Vignettes.
Journal: JAMA Network Open

Data Details: Each data point is a clinical vignette selected from existing literature across medical disciplines such as cardiology, emergency medicine, rheumatology, and dermatology.
Topic: Researchers evaluated the bias of LLM responses across many different demographic factors in different medical subfields.
Note: Different LLM prompts had different outputs, with some on the Likert scale and others being yes or no. Regardless, researchers examined the output and looked for differences in responses.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Likelihood of disease, diagonsis rates, treatment recommendations
Bias Definition: Statstically significant differences in estimation of likelihood of disease, diagonsis rates, treatment recommendations

Title: AI-driven translations for kidney transplant equity in Hispanic populations.
Journal: Nature
Data Source: Frequently asked questions from Organ Procurement and Transplantation Network (OPTN), National Health Service (NHS), and National Kidney Foundation (NKF).
Data Details: Each data point is a frequently asked question about kidney transplantation collected from public health sources such as OPTN, NHS, and the National Kidney Foundation.
Topic: Researchers assess the accuracy of frequently asked questions on kidney health both in linguistic accuracy and cultural sensitivity.
Note: The models' translations where assessed by Mexican native Spanish speaking nephrologists using a rubric with values from 1 to 5. The translates were assessed on linguistic accuracy, which includes "grammatical correctness, appropriate use of vocabulary, and syntactic integrity of the translations, clarity, readability, and technical precision in medical terminology". It was also assessed on cultural sensitivity, which includes "the extent to which translations respected and incorporated cultural nuances, idiomatic expressions, and contextually relevant information for the Hispanic community."
Bias Evaluation Metric: Linguistic accuracy on a Likert scale, cultural sensitivity on a Likert scale
Bias Definition: Statistically significant differences in linguistic accuracy on a Likert scale, statistically significant differences in cultural sensitivity on a Likert scale
Conclusions: Spanish: No

Title: Physician clinical decision modification and bias assessment in a randomized controlled trial of AI assistance.
Journal: Nature
Data Source: There are 2 videos of a standard patient actor depicting a case of chest pain in an outpatient setting, with the actor being either a white male or a black female. 50 physicians are invited to view these videos.
Data Details: Each data point is a physician or resident physicians with training in a general medical specialty (internal medicine, family medicine, or emergency medicine) viewing one of two videos.
Medical Application: Other (Clinician Assistance, Risk Assessment), Triage, Treatment
Topic: Researchers studied how AI assistance affect physician accuracy or bias.
Note: Summary from the paper: "We investigated how doctors respond to AI suggestions when evaluating chest pain, a common but serious medical concern. We showed 50 doctors a video of either a white male or Black female patient describing chest pain symptoms, and asked them to make medical decisions. The doctors then received suggestions from an AI system and could change their decisions. We found that doctors were willing to consider the AI’s suggestions and made more accurate medical decisions after receiving this help. This improvement in decision-making happened equally for all patients, regardless of their race or gender, suggesting AI tools could help improve medical care without increasing bias."
Bias Evaluation Metric: Accuracy of questions
Bias Definition: Statistically significant differences in accuracy of questions
Conclusions: Black: No, White: Yes (Researchers found that participants were more accurate when viewing the Black female video vs. the white male video (p < 0.01) (Supplementary Table 6). The reason for the differing results is unclear but could perhaps be attributed to the Hawthorne effect),

Title: Ensuring Accuracy and Equity in Vaccination Information From ChatGPT and CDC: Mixed-Methods Cross-Language Evaluation.
Journal: JMIR
Data Source: CDC website in both English and Spanish
Data Details: Each datapoint is a frequently asked vaccination-related question on the CDC website, either in English or Spanish. There are 16 questions in each language.
Topic: Researchers measured accuracy, understandability, and readability of ChatGPT's responses to frequently asked vaccination-related questions on the CDC website
Note: There were human readers who assessed the misinformation levels and readability, but also objective system-based metrics for readability, like the Flesch-Kincaid grade level score.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Sentence count, syllable count, understandability, grade level, Flesch-Kincaid readability scores for English and Flesch-Huerta index for Spanish
Bias Definition: Differences in the aforementioned metrics
Conclusions: English: No, Spanish: No

Title: Exploring Biases of Large Language Models in the Field of Mental Health: Comparative Questionnaire Study of the Effect of Gender and Sexual Orientation in Anorexia Nervosa and Bulimia Nervosa Case Vignettes.
Journal: JMIR Mental Health
Data Source: 12 different scientific papers from PubMed and Google Scholar.
Data Details: Each data point is a clinical vignette describing a patient with anorexia nervosa or bulimia nervosa, with variations in gender and sexual orientation used as prompts for LLM evaluation.
Topic: Researchers prompted ChatGPT-4 and MentaLLaMAto score measures of mental health-related quality of life given prompts with different gender and sexual orientations.
Note: ChatGPT-4 showed a gender bias. It rated men as having poorer mental quality of life than equivalent women with the same symptoms. No bias was found for sexual orientation. There are two metrics, like the SF-36 metric that assesses HRQoL and consists of subscales like physical functioning, bodily pain, etc. Also, the eating disorder examination questionnaire (EDE-Q) assesses ED symptomatology, including subscales like dietary restraint and weight concern.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Regression analysis
Bias Definition: Statistically significant features in regression analysis
Conclusions: Female: No, Male: Yes, Heterosexual: No, Homosexual: No

Title: Evaluating for Evidence of Sociodemographic Bias in Conversational AI for Mental Health Support.
Journal: Cyberpsychology, Behavior, and Social Networking
Data Source: Generated Prompts
Data Details: Each data point is a conversation scenario defined by a specific demographic combination, used to prompt ChatGPT-3.5 to simulate a patient complaint and interaction with a conversational agent.
Topic: In this study, we present an approach to investigate the presence of bias within an LLM designed for mental health support.
Note: We simulated physician–patient conversations by using a communication loop between an LLM-based conversational agent and digital standardized patients (DSPs) that engaged the agent in dialogue while remaining agnostic to sociodemographic characteristics. The metric for bias is LIWC, a text analysis software that quantifies psychological and linguistic components in text.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC-22) tool, mean positive tone, mean negative tone
Bias Definition: Statistically significant features in multivariable linear regression for linguistic inquiry and word count (LIWC-22) tool.
Conclusions: Hispanic: No,non hispanic black: No, Non hispanic white: No, Age 40: No, Age 80: No, female: No, male: No, Transgender Female: No, Transgender Male: No, 25000 salary: No, 50000 salary: No, 500,000 salary: No, 1000000 salary: No
Evaluated LLMs: LLM-based program previously developed and tested at Cedars-Sinai Medical Center

Title: Fairness in AI-Driven Oncology: Investigating Racial and Gender Biases in Large Language Models.
Journal: Cureus
Data Source: Generated Prompts
Data Details: Each data point is a prompt describing a healthcare professional in oncology with specific racial and gender demographics, used to evaluate LLM responses about professional roles and recommendations.
Topic: Researchers prompted LLMs with questions related to the roles of oncology professionals based on demographic data.
Note: They compared the outputs of the LLMs to actual healthcare statistics on the demographics of healthcare professionals as well as patient demographics for certain diseases, for the other questions related to certain diseases.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Proportion of recommended ophthalmologists of each identity
Bias Definition: Statistically significant difference in proportion of recommended ophthalmologists from national statistics
Conclusions: Black: Yes (less representation in recommendations), Hispanic: Yes, Native American: Yes, White: Yes, Asian: Yes (less likely to be a part of advocacy and community outreach), Female: Yes, Male: Yes

Title: Mitigating the risk of health inequity exacerbated by large language models.
Journal: npj Digital Medicine
Data Source: They used five datasets. SIGIR 2016 (Proceedings of the 39th International ACM SIGIR conference on Research and Development in Information Retrieval), TREC 2011, and TREC 2022 (Clinical Trial datasets), while the medical question answering, or MQA, datasets are MedQA8 and MedMCQA
Data Details: Each data point is a patient clinical note or medical query augmented with non-decisive socio-demographic factors
Topic: Researchers examined how including socio-demographic factors leads LLMs to biased/mistaken outputs, and proposes mitigation.
Note: Used metric called Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at rank 10. This evaluated performance on tasks related to clinical trial matching and medical question answering.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain, equal opportunity (EO) and demographic parity (DP)
Bias Definition: Differences in aforementioned metrics, researchers evaluated whether certain race, sex and SDOH factors were correlated with similar biased outcomes
Conclusions:
Black: No, Native American: Yes, Middle Eastern: Yes, Pacific Islander: Yes, Homelessness: Yes, unemployment: Yes, Low income: Yes
asian: No, hispanic: No, LGBTQ+: No, Female: No, Male: No
Race / Ethnic Bias: Yes(african american,asian,black,east asian,hispanic,indigenous,middle eastern,native american,mixed race,pacfic islander,south asian,white)

Title: Invisible Bias in GPT-4o-mini: Detecting Disparities in AI-Generated Patient Messaging.
Journal: Journal of Medical Systems
Data Source: Generated
Data Details: Data generated by ChatGPT-4o-mini
Topic: Researchers used an LLM to score LLM-generated patient conversations on empathy, encouragement, accuracy, clarity, professionalism, and respect.
Note: The study also studies demographic divisions along the lines of housing status, including categories like homeless, living alone, living with family, living in a care facility, or living in a shelter. It also studied religious demographics, as well as different marital statuses.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Empathy score, encouragement score, accuracy score, clarity score, professionalism score, respect score
Bias Definition: Statistically significant differences in the aforementioned metrics
Conclusions: Female: Yes (More empathy and encouragement), black: No, east asian: No,hispanic or latino: No,middle eastern: No,south asian: No,white: No, homeless: no,
Race / Ethnic Bias: Yes(black,east asian,hispanic or latino,middle eastern,other,south asian,white)
Other Bias:
Housing (homeless, ‘Living home alone’, ‘Living at home with family’, ‘Care Facility’, ‘Shelter’, and ‘Other’), Religious (‘Christian’, ‘Catholic’, ‘Jewish’, ‘Islamic’, ‘Buddhist’, ‘Hindu’, ‘None or No Preference’, and ‘Other’), Marital Status (‘Married’, ‘Single’, ‘Separated’, ‘Divorced’, and
‘Other’)

Title: Benchmarking bias in embeddings of healthcare AI models: using SD-WEAT for detection and measurement across sensitive populations.
Journal: BMC Medical Informatics and Decision Making
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark / Dataset
Data Source: Other studies

Title: Assessing Racial and Ethnic Bias in Text Generation by Large Language Models for Health Care-Related Tasks: Cross-Sectional Study.

For categorical variables like NER counts and word frequency distributions, researchers used chi-square tests to compare observed frequencies across different races/ethnicities. When the omnibus chi-square test was significant, researchers performed post hoc pairwise chi-square comparisons with Bonferroni correction to identify specific group differences.
Conclusions: African American: No, hispanic: No, White: No, Asian: No

Title: DiversityMedQA: A Benchmark for Assessing Demographic Biases in Medical Diagnosis using Large Language Models

MedQA is a standardized collection
of medical questions from professional medical
board exams. Each data point is a medical question.
Topic: We introduce DiversityMedQA1 , a novel benchmark designed to assess LLM responses to medical queries across diverse patient demographics, such as gender and ethnicity
Note: Most interesting finding: “After qualitative analysis, we noticed that certain words triggered differences in how the models answered questions. Words that related to emotions and actions of distress, such as "crying" or "clutching abdomen", lead to questions being answered differently depending on gender. This behavior is especially pervasive in the GPT3.5 and Llama3-8B models, but only has a minor yet noteworthy presence in the other models. This is likely happening due to the models perpetuating stereotypical differences in how different genders experience pain.”
Bias Evaluation Metric: Accuracy, Accuracy of majority vote of 5 iterations
Bias Definition: Statistically significant differences in accuracy between identity groups found using Z-test.
Conclusions: Male: No, Female: No, African: Yes, Asian: Yes, Caucasian: Yes, Hispanic: Yes, Native American: Yes
Evaluated LLMs: GPT-3.5 Turbo, GPT-4 Turbo, GPT-4o, Gemini 1.5 Flash, and Llama3-8B

Title: Can LLMs Replace Clinical Doctors? Exploring Bias in Disease Diagnosis by Large Language Models
Journal: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: EMNLP 2024
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark or Dataset
Data Source: 336,920 authentic medical records from a hospital
Data Details: Each data point is a medical record from a hospital.
Topic: LLMs Show Systematic Biases in Diagnosis.
Note: Types of Bias: Gender Bias: GPT-4 and Qwen favored male diagnoses; ChatGPT favored female ones. Over-reliance on gender or age in reasoning led to > 98% error rates (page 7, Figure 7). Age Bias: All models biased toward middle-aged patients, reflecting training-data imbalance.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Accuracy, ratio of the number of predicted disease samples to the number of actual samples.
Bias Definition: Disease diagnosis bias is characterized by the disproportionate weight given to certain dimensions of disease, resulting in an unfair skew. Bias in disease diagnosis on a particular dimension as a discrepancy in the model’s inclination towards different values of that dimension.
Conclusions: infant: Yes, children: Yes, teenager: Yes, youth: Yes, middle-aged: No, elderly: Yes, female: Yes, male: Yes
Age Bias: Yes (infant, children, teenager, youth, middle-aged, elderly)

Title: Empirical Study of Social Bias in Medical Question Answering via Large Language Models
Journal: Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare Conference Paper
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark or Dataset
Data Source: College Medicine, Medical Genetics, Professional Medicine datasets from the MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) datasets.
Data Details: This dataset consists of multichoice questions covering many different branches of knowledge. Each data point is a multiple choice question.
Topic: Researchers prompted different LLMs with medical questions and demographic information about race, gender, and professional experience. They asked the LLM to answer the question as if it were someone in those demographic groups. They analyzed performance disparity between the different types of prompts.
Note: Certain types of questions resulted in differences in performance between gender or racial groups. While gender gaps were typically small, the male answers were 3% more accurate in the professional medicine questions. While the racial gaps were not too significant, the Gemma model  saw 4% higher white performance than black on college medicine questions. However, they also saw higher scores often seen for Black prompts in college medicine questions for other models.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Accuracy
Bias Definition: This study quantifies bias as accuracy disparity between different demographics, holding all other variables constant and comparing performance across role information. More specifically, absolute percentage difference in accuracy.
Conclusions: Male: No, Female: Yes, Black: Yes, White: No
Other Bias: Professional Bias (First Year, Second Year, Preclinical, Clinical)

Title: Exposing and Mitigating Calibration Biases and Demographic Unfairness in MLLM Few-Shot In-Context Learning for Medical Image Classification
Journal: Medical Image Computing and Computer Assisted Intervention – MICCAI 2025
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark or Dataset
Data Source: Researchers experimented on three medical imaging datasets: PAPILA for fundus image classification, HAM10000 for skin cancer classification, and MIMIC-CXR for chest X-ray classification.
Data Details: 364 were chosen from PAPILA, 1062 from HAM10000 and 1062 from MIMIC-CXR.
Topic: We introduce CALIN, an inference-time calibration method designed to mitigate the associated biases by estimating the amount of calibration needed.
Note: We consider 5 different metrics in the experiments. (i) classification accuracy, (ii) expected calibration error for quantifying the reliability of predicted confidence, (iii) mean equalized odd ratio between sex and age (EOR) for fairness evaluation, (iv) confidence calibration error gap (CCEG) for calibration fairness evaluation, and (v) equity-scaling measure of calibration error (ESCE) for accessing the overall calibration performance adjusted by subgroups’ performance.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Confidence Calibration Error Gap (CCEG), Equalized Odds Ratio (EOR), Equity-Scaled Calibration Error (ESCE), accuracy,
Bias Definition: Differences in aforementioned metrics, like Confidence Calibration Error Gap
Conclusions: Younger: Yes, Older: Yes, Male: No, Female: No
Debias-details: CALIN operates through a bi-level calibration procedure, effectively mitigating unfairness. Experimental results on three medical imaging datasets demonstrate that CALIN not only enhances fairness but also improves overall predictive performance and exhibits minimum fairnessutility trade-off.

Title: Navigating the Rabbit Hole: Emergent Biases in LLM-Generated Attack Narratives Targeting Mental Health Groups
Journal: COLM 2025 Conference
Data Source: RabbitHole Dataset
Data Details: Each data point is a single toxic generation from Mistral 7B one step in a 15,401-chain corpus of 190,599 total outputs, drawn from the TRH dataset. For each generation, Llama 3.2 3B annotates victim entities (identity groups targeted in the text) and toxicity labels. Those victim entities become nodes in a directed network where edges connect entities appearing in successive generations within the same chain. For the stigmatization analysis, the unit shifts slightly: each data point is a (generation, victim entity) pair, labeled with which of four stigmatization components are present, compared across two positions in the chain the chain's entry point and the first generation containing a mental health entity.
Topic: Investigating stigmas against mental health in LLMs by seeing how close mental health is to negative concepts in network analysis and community detection. Researchers also test if mental health gets brought up in negative scenarios, even when unprompted.
Note: Researchers conduct a centrality analysis of the RabbitHole network that aims to "capture entity transitions across generative chains". They use the Leiden algorithm to analyze this network, and they use a generative chain of prompts to make text more toxic.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Centrality Measure using PageRank, Betweenness, Weighted and Unweighted Degree, Closeness,
Bias Definition: Overrepresented in the toxic output space, they are also more central, more interconnected, and more frequently revisited than other groups, with more stigmatizing framings in language where generative toxicity converges and deepens. This is all compared with the entities in the set not related to mental health.
Conclusions: People with mental health issues: Yes

Journal: Nature Communications Medicine
Data Source: Researchers use PMC-Patients, a large collection of 167,000 patient summaries extracted from case reports in in PubMed Central, as the source to generate synthetic patient profiles. Note that they did not include the original data in their experimentation, as they used AI to remove demographic data and pre treatment data.
Data Details: Each data point is a patient summary from case reports in in PubMed Central with the pre-treatment details and demographic details removed. Instead, one of four hypothetical race labels were added. Originally, researchers extracted a large collection of 167,000 patient summaries extracted from case reports in in PubMed Central, but they narrowed it down heavily.
Topic: Quantitative analysis of racial bias in medical report generation
Note: There was a focus on racial bias measurement, using pair-wise win rate to compare two racial groups at once. Notably, the model favored white patients with superior and immediate treatments,  longer hospitalization stays, and better recovery outcomes, which is also reflected in the higher projected cost.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Presence of hallucinations, physical examination results, pathological test results, treatment plan, expected cost without insurance, patient description, case history, expected outcome of the treatment plan, actual outcome, hospitalized days, survival rate
Bias Definition: Presence of hallucinations for certain identity groups, differences in the aforementioned qualities, pairwise win rates
Conclusions: White: Yes, Black: Yes, Asian: Yes, Hispanic: Yes

Title: Uncovering Judgment Biases in Emergency Triage: A Public Health Approach Based on Large Language Models
Journal: Proceedings of the 4th Machine Learning for Health Symposium, PMLR 259, pages 420-439, 2025
Data Source: Researchers collected reports from approximately 480,000 entries to the Adult ED of the Bordeaux University Hospital.
Data Details: Each data point is a report containing the exact moment of the admission, the sex of the patient, the chief complaint, history of present illness, past medical history, an array of vital signs (heart rate, respiratory rate, blood pressure, among others), and the associated triage score.
Topic: Judgment bias in emergency triage decision-making by LLMs
Note: Researchers used typical data science accuracy measures for classification tasks like accuracy score, F1, recall, and precision.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Predicted traige score, precision, recall, F1
Bias Definition: Differences between predicted triage score and actual triage score from a nurse.
Conclusions: Female: Yes, Male: No

Title: ChatGPT for Mental Health Applications: A study on biases.
Journal: AIMLSystems '23: Proceedings of the Third International Conference on AI-ML Systems
Data Source: Reddit based datasets: CLPsych 2019, Dreaddit, Suicide Risk Dataset
Data Details: Mental health scenarios and counseling prompts
Topic: Researchers study biases in mental health applications of ChatGPT
Note: They want to focus on suicide prevention and understand which groups are more protected or at risk when interacting with ChatGPT3.5 Turbo.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Accuracy, F1 score
Bias Definition: Differences in accuracy and F1 score in comparison to control group
Conclusions: Male: Yes, Female: No, Transgender: Yes, Gender Nonconforming: Yes, Age 18 to 30: No, Age 31 to 50, No, Age 51+: Yes,

Title: Africa Health Check: Probing Cultural Bias in Medical LLMs
Journal: Proceedings of the 2025 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark / Dataset
Data Source: PubMed Corpus, then reviewed by researchers.
Data Details: The multiple choice questions that they ultimately generated fell into three variants: No-context questions simply ask for the most appropriate answer without providing patient details, location, or cultural information. Full-Context questions do provide this information. Misleading-Context questions are similar to Full-Context questions, but they contain deceptive cultural cues.
Topic: Researchers study how culturally adapted LLMs are in considering traditional medicine, in particular traditional African herbal medicine. Medical LLMs are normally biased to allopathic, or Western, treatment options.
Note: The unique contribution of this paper is the Cultural Bias Attribution metric, which quantifies how input prompts drive culturally biased responses. They also create their own dataset through a systematic review of PubMed and they prompt PubMed with this. They use ChatGPT-4o as part of the MCQ generation pipeline.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Cultural bias score, cultural bias attribution, traditional medicine accuracy, allopathic medicine accuracy
Bias Definition: Differences in cultural bias score and cultural bias attribution
Conclusions: African: Yes (some models showed inherent preferences for Western, or allopathic, medicine over traditional African medicine), Western: No
Debias-details: Different inference methods, like Zero-Shot, Few-Shot, and Instruction-Tuning were compared along the metrics of cultural bias attribution and cultural bias score.

Title: Towards conversational assistants for health applications: using ChatGPT to generate conversations about heart failure
Journal: Proceedings of the 26th Annual Meeting of the Special Interest Group on Discourse and Dialogue
Data Source: Generated
Data Details: The data is separated into several folders, aave, domain, reason-prompt, reasoning, and sdoh. Within these folders are files containing conversations between patients and healthcare specialists. Each data point is a simulated conversation between a patient and a healthcare professional.
Topic: Researchers test the ability of prominent commercial LLMs to generate patient-health educator dialogues while incorporating African American Vernacular English and also Social Determinants of Health.
Note: They concluded that despite the different prompting methods improving output quality, "ChatGPT still lacks the empathy and engagement" necessary for effective communication in healthcare. They also included human reviewers, who rated randomly selected generated conversations. The reviewers read 3 conversations for each prompting method and completed a Likert scale style questionnaire. They also used metrics like round adherence rate, follow-up rate, ratio of words, and format adherence rate.
Bias Evaluation Metric:
Human reviewers rated appropriateness on 5-point Likert scale, human reviewers rated pereived empathy on 5-point Likert scale, human reviewers rated tailorization according to SDOH on 5-point Likert scale, round adherence ratio: ratio of how often the generated conversation matched the number of conversational rounds in the prompt instructions, format adherence ratio: ratio of how often the generated conversation format matched the format described in the prompt instructions, follow-up ratio: the number of
follow-up questions asked by the PE to that by the patient
Bias Definition: Differences in the aforementioned metrics
Conclusions: AAVE: No, standard English: No, Young: No, Mid-age: No, Old: No, Female: No, Male: No, Unsafe Neighborhoods: Yes (Researchers found instances where models lacked empathy in their response towards unsafe neighborhoods.), Safe Neighborhoods: No, below poverty socio-economic conditions: No, well to do socio-economic conditions: No
Other Bias:
Neighborhood bias (Safe, Unsafe), Socio-economic conditions (below poverty
line, well to do)
Debias-details: Researchers used prompting methods described as: aspects, African American Vernacular English, Social Determinants of Health (SDOH), and SDOH-informed reasoning.

Title: EMPEC: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Models Across Diverse Healthcare Professions
Journal: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL 2025
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark / Dataset
Data Source: Past exams for healthcare professionals in the Professional and Technical Examinations of Taiwan and Republic of China. Can see more here: https://wwwq.moex.gov.tw/exam/wFrmExamQandASearch.aspx
Data Details: Each data point is a multiple choice question from "officially published past exams for healthcare professionals in the Professional and Technical Examinations of Taiwan, Republic of China". These questions were written to assess medical knowledge and clinical skills for a diverse array of healthcare professions. Note that they used ChatGPT and other software to translate the dataset into other languages.
Topic: Researchers introduce a new dataset called Examinations of Medical PErsonnel in Chinese (EMPEC), which they use to evaluate the knowledge and performance of LLMs across underrepresented healthcare professions like Optometrists and Audiologists.
Note: ChatGPT-4 had the highest performance in 19 out of the 20 medical professions tested, but Qwen1.5-70B-Chat achieves highest accuracy in the Traditional Chinese Medicine Practitioner domain. Models trained in English benefit from an English translation. Despite using different tokens, Traditional and Simplified Chinese have similar performance, improving the accuracy of some models while decreasing for others.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Accuracy
Bias Definition: Differences in accuracy
Conclusions: English: No, Simplified Chinese: No, Traditional Chinese: No

Title: Polish-English medical knowledge transfer: A new benchmark and results
Journal: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: EMNLP 2025
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark / Dataset
Data Source:
Researches scrape past exams given to Polish physicians and dentists from 2008 to 2024, categorized by specialization. This includes the  LEK (Lekarski Egzamin Koncowy, Medical ´
Final Examination), LDEK (Lekarsko-Dentystyczny Egzamin
Koncowy, Dental Final Examination), and the PES (Panstwowy Egzamin Specjalizacyjny, National Specialization Examination). Both the LEK and LDEK were translated into English.
Data Details: Each data point is a multiple choice question from one of the exams described in the data source field: LEK, LDEK, PES.
Topic: Researchers tested the ability of LLMs to answer medical examination questions from various Polish exams. Many questions were translated into English as well, and the results between different models, types of questions, and language of questions.
Note: Results were assessed in terms of accuracy on the exam questions. The study comapred percentages of correct answers across different exams and languages. Model performance was also compared to average human performance. Note that English translations of the original material in Polish was provided by human experts. A key takeaway from the study's observations is that language transfer could be more effective as general model performance scales up.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Accuracy, binary variable for passing score or not
Bias Definition: Differences in the aforementioned metrics
Conclusions: English: No, Polish: Yes

Title: Evaluating Large Language Models on Health-Related Claims Across Arabic Dialects
Journal: Proceedings of the 1st Workshop on NLP for Languages Using Arabic Script
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark / Dataset
Data Source: Researchers used the AraFacts dataset, which contains natural claims which have been fact-checked.
Data Details: Each data point is a question create through a question generation pipeline, where natural claims fact-checked by a fact-checked website like Fatabyyano or Misbar are feed into ChatGPT and created into question-answer pairs in different dialects of Arabic.
Topic: Researchers measure how LLMs react to health claims written in different Arabic dialects. They want to see how well subtle cultural and linguistic details are accounted for, and how models can be accurate and culturally sensitive in their response.
Note: Researchers also used a metric called the cultural sensitivity analyzer, which measured how well models adapted their responses to different dialects.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Factual Accuracy, agreement distribution, cultural sensitivity score, consistency valuation, eeasoning analysis (the depth and quality of the model’s reasoning in making a decision)
Bias Definition: Differences in the aforementioned metrics
Conclusions: Lebanese: Yes, Saudi Arabic: No, Egyptian: No, Morrocan: No
Race / Ethnic Bias: Polish: Yes (lower accuracy and weaker knowledge transfer), English: No
Language Bias: Yes(Saudi Arabic, Egyptian Arabic, Lebanese Arabic, Moroccan Arabic)

Title: Towards Algorithmic Fidelity: Mental Health Representation across Demographics in Synthetic vs. Human-generated Data
Journal: Proceedings of the 2024 Joint International Conference on Computational Linguistics, Language Resources and Evaluation (LREC-COLING 2024)
Paper Type: Benchmark / Dataset, Experiment on Existing Models
Data Source: Generated
Data Details: Another study, called asked patients to describe the largest source of stress in their life and how they dealt with that stress. They used this human generated data, and then they augmented the data set with ChatGPT-3.
Topic: Researchers prompt LLMs to provide significant significant stressors in people's lives depending on their demographic identities. They then conduct pairwise analysis of how often certain issues are associated with certain demographic groups.
Note: They found that existing stereotypes were reinforced. Women were often stressed about health, news and social media, news and politics, family, and relationships. Men were more concerned about finances and unemployment, racism, and police brutality. Along racial lines, African Americans more associated with bio, health, body, percept, racism, and police brutality. Asians more associated with perfectionism, comparing to others, work, school, and reward. Hispanics more concerned with immigration, family, social interactions, etc. Whites more associated with general stress, news and social media, relationships, and uncertainty.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Structural topic model analysis generates vectors encoded with racial and gendered information
Bias Definition: Cosine similarity between the vectors
Conclusions: Hispanic: Yes, Asian: Yes, Black: Yes, White: No, Women: Yes, Men: Yes

Title: What Goes In, Must Come Out: Generative Artificial Intelligence Does Not Present Algorithmic Bias Across Race and Gender in Medical Residency Specialties
Journal: Cureus
Data Source: American Academy of Medical Colleges (AAMC) list of medical professions.
Data Details: Researchers found a list of medical professions from the AAMC website and selected 19.
Topic: Researchers study representation of medical professionals across diverse specialties in the image generating model DALL-E2.
Note: They found that OpenAI's DALL-E2's predictions generally aligned with the current demographic landscape of medical residents. This suggests that the output was mirroring the training data rather than projecting algorithmic bias into the output.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Generated images labeled with race, generated images labeled with a gender
Bias Definition: Statistically significant differences in racial distribution in labeled images separated by profession and racial distribution of residents as reported by the American Academy of Medical Colleges in 2021-2022 separated by profession
Conclusions: Male: No, Female: No, Asian: No, Black: No, Hispanic: No, White: No
Race / Ethnic Bias: Yes(Asian, Black or African American, Hispanic or Latino or of Spanish origin, white)
